rubric_notes: Synthesis rubric extracted from existing rubric files. Original rubric content is preserved; question IDs are prefixed to avoid collisions across exams.

questions.grading_note: Evaluate the student's solution against ONLY ONE of the two valid algorithmic approaches below: Track A (DFS + Memoization) OR Track B (Sorting + Bottom-Up DP). Choose the track that best matches the student's logic. Do not combine points from both tracks. A highly inefficient brute-force attempt receives maximum 5 points.

criteria.description: Track A (DFS+Memo) - State Exploration: Establishes standard Depth-First Search logic exploring valid 4-directional moves that are strictly strictly greater/smaller than the current cell.

Track A (DFS+Memo) - Memorization: Implements a DP table/memo dictionary to cache results, returning the cached value immediately if a cell has already been visited to prevent exponential re-evaluations.
Track A (DFS+Memo) - Complexity: Achieves or explicitly states an optimal time complexity of O(m*n).
Track B (Sorting) - Initialization & Sorting: Correctly extracts all cells into a list (storing value, row, col) and sorts them by value.
Track B (Sorting) - DP State Updates: Iterates through the sorted list and checks valid 4-directional neighbors, updating a DP table by adding 1 to the path length of the valid neighboring cells.
Track B (Sorting) - Complexity: Recognizes that the time complexity is bottlenecked by the sorting step, stating it as O(mn log(mn)).

questions.grading_note: Targeted calibration. Score bands: 20 only if grammar captures exactly b*(abb*)* (allows all valid strings and excludes clear invalid ones such as isolated a without required following b). About 10 when one major idea is correct but grammar is incomplete/overgenerating. Around 5 only for fragmentary attempts with at least one relevant production idea. Prioritize language behavior over variable names.

criteria.description: Core correctness: grammar enforces that every generated a must be followed by at least one b (the essential abb* constraint), avoiding obvious invalid strings with unconstrained lone a.
Completeness: grammar also supports the leading b* portion and repeated block behavior (abb*)* with workable recursion/termination so valid strings across the language family are generable.

questions.grading_note: Professor pattern is coarse-banded: 20 only for both correct language and clear unambiguity; 10 for language-correct but ambiguous; around 4-5 for partly aligned structure; 0 for clearly wrong language.

criteria.description: CFG correctly generates the language L={a^i b^j | j <= i <= 2j}. If ambiguous but language-correct, cap near 10 total before unambiguity criterion.
CFG is strictly unambiguous (every string in the language has exactly one leftmost derivation or parse tree)

questions.grading_note: Full credit requires all three components: certificate definition, verifier description, and polynomial-time argument. Partial credit is awarded for each correct component independently.

criteria.description: Correctly defines the certificate: a subset of items (i.e., which items are selected for the knapsack)
Correctly describes the verifier: checks that the total weight of the selected items does not exceed the weight limit L, and that the total value meets or exceeds the target K
Correctly argues polynomial time: summing weights and values over the selected subset takes O(n) time where n is the number of items
Clearly states the conclusion: since a polynomial-time verifier exists, Knapsack is in NP
Bonus: correctly notes the certificate size is polynomial (a subset of n items is representable in O(n) bits)

questions.bonus_note: q3_5 is a bonus criterion worth up to 2 points; total is capped at 16.

questions.grading_note: A complete reduction proof requires four components: the construction, the forward direction, the backward direction, and the polynomial-time argument. Missing any direction of correctness loses significant points. Award partial credit if construction is correct but one direction of proof is missing or flawed.

criteria.description: Correct construction: given G = (V, E) with |V| = n, construct G' as two disjoint copies of G with no edges between them, giving |V'| = 2n

Forward direction (HAMILTONIAN → TONIAN): if G has a Hamiltonian path, then one copy of G in G' has a path visiting all n vertices, which is exactly n = |V'|/2 vertices, satisfying the Tonian condition

Backward direction (TONIAN → HAMILTONIAN): if G' has a Tonian path visiting >= n vertices, and the two copies are disconnected, the path must lie entirely within one copy of G (which has exactly n vertices), so the path visits all n vertices — a Hamiltonian path in G
Polynomial-time argument: the construction (copying V and E twice) runs in O(|V| + |E|) time
Clear conclusion: since the construction is polynomial and the two instances are equivalent (G has a Hamiltonian path iff G' has a Tonian path), HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN
